He was a Clerk Typist, and he was a wiz at computers. And he documented everything, and all that stuff is at Anderson Street. So circle back to my Anderson Street thing. We need to get it out of there. We need to find the little tiny discs thing, before you can't. I mean, you'll always be able to find something on eBay. But, you know, certainly we need, we need that documentation, just information dumped. And then somebody can come along and refine it. I'm down with doing it, but, you know. Yeah, but you need, it's best, yes. Yeah. It would be nice if you had a team. If I had a team. Yeah. So just giving you the half an hour warning. There's the El Sereno Branch Library. El Sereno. Okay. El Sereno, we moved from one place to another up the block. Because it's always been on Huntington? Huntington Drive South. Not Huntington, but Huntington Drive South. And you have to have the designation or you'll get lost. But the original El Sereno was a bond branch, and we bought property up the street. I mean, it's literally a block away from where it used to be. And that was an interesting move, to move from one to another. Yeah, same side of the street. Excuse me, let me take a sip of water. You see [unintelligible]. Yeah, same side of the street just about a block away. So that was an easy move, technically. Yeah, but, but we were still boxing at that point, I think. But it was fairly simple to do. I don't remember anything really weird about it. Just that we were moving. It was a very small bond branch, and it's really a lovely big building now. It's like the late nineties? Yeah. It's got the community room. You walk through the front door and it's got the community room. It's the only branch I found that doesn't have a dedication plaque, which is weird. Oh that is interesting. Which is really weird because I call the branch and I was looking for—at one point, they were stealing the dedication plaques off the outside of the buildings. Why? Just for their content, metal content? Brass, yeah. Oh, good gravy. Good gravy. Yeah, the story about the Watts one is hysterical. They stole the, they stole the plaque. And the plaque is huge off the side of the Watts branch. And so we get—PD gets a call from this guy who says that somebody had come— He's holding it hostage? No. That somebody had come, that someone had come to his scrapyard to sell it. And he knew it was stolen because obviously it wasn't scrap metal. So he said he told the guy that he didn't have the cash today. Please come back tomorrow. So the guy comes back— And they put him in the— PD is there and they arrest him. Oh that's great. And we got our plaque back. We were so happy. Yeah, we were so happy. Oh, that's a great story. What year was that roughly? Very recent actually, within the last two or three years. Well, that's when we lost the Peace Bell down here, too. Well, they ran over the Peace Bell. No, but the metal was still there. And then I heard the bell disappeared. No, no, it's still there. So there you go, the rumor. Yeah. I think the Peace Bell, we rolled it into the parking lot. It's still there. Oh, okay. Yeah, we, we, we hid it. Oh, see, good, we're straightening out all sorts of— We moved it. No we didn't—they didn't steal the Peace Bell. Oh, that's good. The Peace Bell was too heavy to steal. I was going to say. No, no, no, no. No, it's huge. And they needed a forklift to pick it up. I mean, the fact that the guy on a stolen forklift knocked it over has something to do with it, too. That's what happened. Somebody stole a forklift from some site down the street, and was joyriding it down the street. He was in his cups, I think, and—you know that expression? Oh, it must have, it might have been from the Metro, uh, extension. Yeah. Whatever it was, whatever it was, he stole a forklift and just— Went on down. —went on down, and drove across the grass and hit the Peace Bell and knocked it over and got stuck in the, in the mud. Oh, I didn't know. Yeah. Yeah, yeah. So back to El Sereno. Okay, back to El Sereno. Was the honey man there? Do you remember him? Because he's been right next door to the Huntington branch for, like, fifty years selling honey. No, I don't remember him. Okay. No problem. But that's, but the El Sereno branch is, there's nothing particular about the move or anything? No, nothing. Nothing—I mean, it went fairly smooth. It was just another one of those moves where people came from other—a lot of these moves were done very joyously. I will say this, and I might as well say it now, people from other branches came to help us pack out. That is interesting. Oh, yeah. So were they, were they employees? Sure, sure. But they asked people to volunteer to come. Oh, nice. And everybody came. I mean, they got paid to do it, but they came to help us pack. And people from other branches came to help us pack, because many hands make light work. Well, that's right, but to get the staffing. Now then, I say this and I'll probably get in trouble for this one too. I hope so. I hope you tell more things that might get you in trouble. We couldn't do it today, because people will not volunteer. I mean, the joyfulness of being able to do something, and to step out of your comfort zone, to work with other people, to build something, to move something, to be a part of this joy. I don't understand why people don't want to do things that are outside their little box of things they think are theirs to do. So you don't have any theories why that, or where that changed or? I think it's a mindset. I think, I think the mindset changed. When did you notice that change? I don't know. You know, I'm a hippie. I grew up in the sixties. My parents were always, "Pitch in. You have to pitch in. You can't just stand around watching people work." And I think there's a whole different mindset of people who don't want to do anything except what they're supposed to do. Come in, do their shtick, and leave. For me, the library is a mission. To me, what we do is an amazing thing that we give every single citizen, every single person in the city of Los Angeles access to things they don't have access to. We impact everyone. Every single human being who lives, works, or visits the city of Los Angeles has the opportunity to come into a library and get what they need to know. And it just fries me that people—I can't get people to help me, you know? Like I can, because I will force you to help me, but because I, I lead by example, I'm not going to stand there and point. I'm not a magic finger person. I'm down in the dirt with you. But you got to help me. But I don't think the mindset is to do these things. I, honest to God, don't see people volunteering to— So you don't remember a particular time when that might have changed? No, I'm not going to say it came with any kind of administration. I think, I think there was a shift, a generational shift. All right. And, you know, maybe it's not nice. I mean, my daughter, I make my—I used to make my daughter volunteer. "You're coming with me." But I don't see volunteerism as a motivation anymore. Right. You know, what am I going to get out of it? What are you going to get out of it is the joy of helping someone. You know, when I, when I work at a rescue mission and I'm handing someone a plate of food, the joy is giving that person food. You know, the joy is the human connection that you're making with other people. That's right. But, you know, I'm a hippie. It is a big part of public libraries. It is, and it is, and it's one of the reasons I work at the public library. I've worked in other places. Yeah. So I don't want to—I mean, we've got about ten minutes left, so I want to talk about Encino-Tarzana. Ah, Encino. Encino, which [unintelligible]. I've known people that have worked there. And it's not a trouble free branch in terms of the population, I've heard. No. Encino is a— I wonder if you could say that about every branch, but still. Well, sure. I mean, every branch has their moments of whatever this is. (both laugh) Encino was a tear down and rebuild on the same spot. Okay. Okay. But the problem— Because they were a bond branch of '59? Yeah. The '57. Fifty-seven bond. So—and that was a dinky, tiny little building. But Encino is built—so there's a thruway in between the parking lot that belongs to the county. Access to the what we'll call "the wash", the LA River. But, you know, as a kid growing up there, you called it "the wash." So, so we couldn't fence off our parking lot. Can't do it, because the strip in the middle of it belongs to the county. True that. So there are issues with people who— Is that an easement? What is— It's an easement. Yeah. It belongs to the county. It belongs to the county. And because we bought— Couldn't sneak that past them. Because it's Public, they are Public Works Department [unintelligible]. Because we bought the building, the property behind it and tore down [unintelligble]. Before that, it wasn't a problem because we butted up against the easement of the county. That's where our parking lot was. But when we bought the lot next door we had to allow access to the wash. I don't know what they call it because I'm a Valley girl. We call it the wash. So, it's a problem with people who want to live against the fence there, that butts up against the wash. And we do have homeless population in that community, and it's there. The day we brought the collection in that had been purchased, the additional collection from Baker & Taylor, it was pouring gangbusters. Wow. We found the leak in the roof. Oh. (laughs) Because I walked in, I walked in, and—I'm such a smart aleck—and all along the back wall, it was just coming down in sheets, right. It was raining and I went, "Oh, water feature?" They were not happy with me. So this is a thing, though, isn't it? How many branches have like—because I remember, the branch I was hired to work at had leaks and it was a new, new construction. So what, how many of them have had leaks? Because the theory is, right, they don't build for water, because— Because it doesn't exist. You know, most of the time when we open a branch, it's a see change, right? So you're waiting for the, for the first storm to see where the leaks are. Because they will be there. They will be. They just are. Even when they were building underground here, they found that out. Maybe more so because it was underground. Yeah, well, there's going to be leaks. There will be, you know, it's just, it just is. Because even if they water test the roof—water testing, they get up there with a hose. But when you have a torrential rainstorm— It gets everywhere. Yeah, yeah. You know, the pounding on the roof and the ice that's falling and everything else. Because you have hailstorms, especially in the Valley. You get hailstorms. Horrible. And in South Central, which is random. Yeah, random. But we were—the poor guy who was bringing in the collection, he was wetter than wet, and we couldn't bring the pallets into the building because everything was soaked. So at the time, there was a strip at the entryway where there was no tile and no anything. So I had him bring them in two pallets at a time, and the guys were breaking down and bringing the boxes in and emptying. It was crazy, but it was raining so hard you couldn't see your hand in front of your face. And that's what I remember about Encino-Tarzana. Every time I pass it, I think, "Oh yeah, there was that rainstorm." PTSD for rain. Yeah. Yeah. Well, you know, and the thing is that— It is. It's a terrible thing. Water is like, as bad as fire is for both. You know, I'm fairly adaptable and I'm quick to think about, "Okay, well, we can't do this. We gotta do this." My mind makes those kinds of leaps, which is why I'm really suited for this kind of job. Because I pivot really fast—oh sorry, hit the mic—but I pivot really fast. And so that's what we did. But it was crazy nuts. And the building itself— So what was the pivot you made? I brought things into the lobby and emptied into the lobby rather than bring everything in at once, because I didn't want to bring the wet things onto the carpet. Oh, yeah. Because there's a tile entryway and immediately carpet. It might have, might even have affected the new, the adhesive for the carpet. Yeah. No, I don't, you know, you got, you know, possible mold issues. You got possible, possible destruction issues because if it gets wet, you know, if the—no, mm-hmm . So that's what we did. We brought it in like that. And the building itself was really controversial. If you've seen it, there's that peak that goes out and it appears to go out over Ventura Boulevard which it really doesn't. It's an optical illusion. But people were just beside themselves about it. It's an entry on the corner on that one too isn't it? No it's not, that's not the entry. Oh, that's just the leading edge. Just the leading edge of the building. Because the entry, the parking lot is on one side. And the entry, you got to come around it. That's the way the parking lot is. And it's kind of funky. Kind of weird, the way they set the parking lot. But I think it's because of the county easement and all of the where we built. So we built on our side of the easement, and then the parking lot is partially— Right, and people didn't like the—the problem with the, they thought that it was going out over the, the the leading edge was going out over Ventura Boulevard. Yeah, yeah. And they just freaked out. They freaked out. Oh, people were complaining. But what was the nature of their complaint? It gets in the way? They they thought it would, since it was over the street, that it would be hit by trucks. But it's not even—okay. It's not. How far up is it? It's like twenty-five feet. Yeah, but it's not even on the street. It doesn't hang out over the street. But even if it were, it's really tall— Yeah, I mean— Bridges, bridges are fifteen, like, at a minimum. Yeah, I know. I know, but, but— People that, that actually, there's probably—yes. That community is, is—I don't know, every community has people that— Every community has people who think that they—and, you know, they got their input. They're good. We'll listen to it. "Thank you. Thank you for your input." Because they saw the plans presumably too. But I did have a question about how, how did the easement really affect, like, you couldn't, it certainly affected the the footprint of where the building could be. But what other ways did it, did it affect? We can't put a gate around the parking lot. So people park in the parking lot all night. There you go. So it becomes an attractive hazard with a parking lot. And we've done what we can to mitigate it and work with the community. And it's not, I don't think it's as bad as it once was because— but then again, I'm not— Do you have security? Does security go? That's a different question. No Okay. Yeah. Because they don't— No, we don't. Unless there's fire watch or something. Right. We don't, we don't have enough security to send out on patrol. They used to. But the dynamics of our security team has changed, and they are needed for other things. And so they don't have the night shift that they used to. Anything else about Encino? Because the next one might take a minute. And we're kind of, and I think we should probably end with the next one. Anything more about Encino? No. Expo. Oh, Expo. Old and new. Old and new. Exposition Park. When I came in 2006, they were just moving in to the new building. Okay. No they weren't. No they weren't. They moved in 2008. Yeah. Two years, okay. Yeah, because, yeah. I remember 2008. And they're number 71. No, they're not. They're number 6. They have the old Expo number. They buried that number. I don't care what anybody calls it. It's number 6. That's right. It used to be, when it was the University branch. Right. It was University branch to begin with. And then we sold that property to the University and moved on to Vermont. Three, six, six, five. Because it was on University Place even, I think. Right, right. It was on—and as the university expanded and needed the space, we sold and we moved to 3665 South Vermont. And that was also, I believe, a bond branch, because in my mind I'm thinking it was—it looked like a bond. I remember it, it was brick, like, and it was east, it was east facing. But there was like a big— what was, there was a huge space behind it. Like an open space. Parking lot. Parking lot like this. Oh, a big parking lot. Yeah. Now, the thing about the parking lot was when, when there were things going on at 'SC or the Rose Bowl, people would sell spaces in the parking lot. They'd take the gates off the parking lot and sell spaces because we weren't open on Sundays back then. That's right. Now Expo is open on Sundays. Right. But honestly, they were doing something on a Saturday, and the parking lot was filling up mysteriously. And do you remember Betsy Hoag, my boss, right? I loved working for Betsy. She was the branch manager at Expo at the time, and she goes out to the parking lot, and there's this guy selling— This must have been in the mideighties. Yeah, selling spaces in the parking lot. How much? (laughs) I don't know. I don't know. But she said, "What are you doing?" Because there's a game, right? Yeah. There's a game. Right. Right. That's fantastic. So, you know, the Coliseum. Um— And he's like, "Who are you?" And he goes, "The guy who runs the building told me I could do this." And she looks at him and she goes, "I'm the guy who runs the building. And I didn't give you permission." (laughs) That's fantastic. There probably was a guy that said he ran the building that did give him permission. Had nothing to do with the library. (laughs) Yeah. He just, he just, he just decided that it had to be a guy running the building. Of course. But people would come back and their cars would be towed and impounded because, you know. There are signs. There are signs. Don't, you know, you can't sell parking. And then the university got that building too, right? Yeah. They did. And they tore it down. I don't know what's there now, but they tore that building down. Yeah. And then now it moved into that beautiful LEED [Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design] building. Yeah. It's a beautiful building. Yeah, it really is a lovely building. And we did move the, um, the Charles White mural— Yes. Let's talk about that. —into the new building. That is a big deal. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, it's historic. And— Oh, my God, it must have been a nightmare. You had to be so careful. Yeah. They did. Crest movers still came? Oh, no, no, no, no, no, no, mmm-hmm. (Jim laughs) It was done, it was done by someone—by art preservation people who knew what they were doing. Thank God, because I wouldn't let Crest movers anywhere near it. No, thank you. I mean, I love, I love my guys, but no thank you. Oh my gosh. No. But yeah, they moved that into the new building and they restored it. How do you move a mural like that? Very carefully. Very carefully, I suppose. I was not involved in the move of that. I just know they did it. And it's just stunning when you walk into the new building and it's there above the circulation desk. Just gorgeous. And that's one of our LEED, one of our LEED buildings. And my theory too, is that they built that far away from USC so that they could have it for more than twenty years. (laughs) Yeah. And as part of Rec and Parks— It's on Vermont and Thir— Western. It's on Western. Um, Western and 24th? Yeah, yep. But we're in the park there, which causes a lot of issues, because it's a city park. Something—and the Dodgers sponsor part of the park. Yeah, or did at one point. They sponsor the batting, the softball field, the baseball field. You know, it's problematic because of the location in a park and the homeless population. Definitely not the only branch like that. I mean, you have, you know, North Hollywood and— Oh, yeah. Pico-Union and, yeah. Yeah. Pico-U, not so much. Pico-U isn't in a park. But it's right—oh, okay. But it's right next door to MacArthur— not Pico-Union, what's? That's what I thought. I'm getting it wrong. No, I don't know which one you're thinking of. Off MacArthur Park. Oh. Felipe de Neve. Felipe de Neve. Sorry. Forgive me, Felipe. It's okay. It's okay. I mean, it could make you think of it as—that one is interesting, a very interesting building. And that's coming up pretty soon. That's coming up. And we'll talk about, yeah. Anything else you want to say about Exposition Park? The current branch, the old branch? Yeah. I mean, the old branch was interesting, but— Did you get a lot of USC students, too? Oh, probably. Oh, sure. Sure. Yeah, a lot of, a lot of college kids there. And it's funny because Northridge Branch is right across from Northridge Library. I don't get a lot of college students. I didn't even know it was there when I went to college. It said CSUN, which tells you how I was, but they have big, big libraries there. So I would assume that they would get, would have gotten some, but not a lot because I think, the college has several large libraries, but I'm assuming they got college kids. Not so much, because I was in and out of there. It used to be the regional branch. So the regional manager was there. Still regional branch? Right? Not anymore. No, but the regional manager was in and out of there. What is the West Valley's regional? It's, not West Valley? No, no, no, no, no, no. Hang on— I forgot, two. —Hang on, there are two. There are two, right. Right. Sort of like Central Southern. You've got the San Pedro, but also kind of J Serra [Juniper Serra] is the backup. No. J Serra isn't. Well, it was at one point because it had that central office— It had an office, it was an office, but it was never a designated place. Now, now my brains are addled, right? Okay. Probably a good time to stop. (laughs) Probably a good time to stop. My brain shut off. But Expo is great, but it's new. And was there anything particular about the move besides the enormous, you know, mural? No. It went, it was one of those smooth moves. It was easy to do. Because it went, yeah, just up the street. We did that one on book trucks. That was on book trucks. Boom boom, in and out. Or, not book trucks. Machine carts. So we did that all on machine carts. We were—and we were in and out in no time. That's the way to move from existing to existing. It's the only way to do it. Don't box anything. Yeah. It slows everything down. Yeah, but I will share a moving tip with you. Please. Okay. If you are moving and you're boxing things, don't write on the box. Use color. You know, red dots, this bedroom. Because your eye will process color quicker than it will process a word. You can write on the box, but don't lean on that because, because then you'll know immediately, "Oh, that box doesn't belong here." And you'll learn it like within, within ten minutes. You know exactly, you don't even have to think about it. That color goes there. And if you're having people move you, you draw a little blueprint of the floor and then you put the dots like— There you go, professional. So you're not, so people aren't asking you constantly, "Where do the red boxes go? Where do the blue boxes go?" "Where's the living room." Yeah. "Is your living room the same as this family room?" Yeah. Yeah, yeah. You just put the dot on the dot and you're done. Just, that's a moving tip that, well, it's one of those things I share with people that are moving. I have a friend who was moving, you know, and I gave him that tip because he was moving from Lubbock, Texas, to somewhere in the Midwest. I forget where. And I said, "Dude, do this." And he snottily told me—because he's a teacher—he goes, "All my friends know how to read." (Jim laughs) And I said, "Not at the end of the day, they don't." But he just did it. He messaged me back. He goes, "You were right." (laughs) There you go, there you go. I said, "I'm a professional. I've done this before." (Jim laughs) You do it in your sleep. You could do it in your sleep. Well, Robyn, as usual, it's always great talking to you. We did ten. We got ten branches which I think is a lot. But we also the stuff in between is important too. We talk about all the stuff that, that helps, you know, get into some of the more minutia, the granularity that's important for people like we're talking about, that are looking in the future of how to do this stuff. You know, heaven forfend, we would have to do that anytime soon. But you know it's going to happen at some point. But at some point we'll do more than one. At some point we'll start, because I know there are plans to do other libraries. You know, there— That's a whole other— —there is a forward thinking. Where's the next technical branch? Like, technically, what would be the next branch? Arleta. Interesting. I see a lot of about Arleta. Do you know where Arleta is? Okay. It's between, it's between Pacoima and Panorama City. That's interesting. So they're looking at that. So let's leave it at that. I want to—because that actually sounds like a very interesting conversation all in itself. So thank you once again. You're welcome. And we'll make this happen again soon because we got to get to Fairfax. We got through the Es, we got to get to the Fs. Oh gosh. Fairfax. No that's great. Thank you so much, Robyn. You're welcome. My pleasure.

organizations.name: Los Angeles Public Library
organizations.description: The Los Angeles Public Library provides free and easy access to information, ideas, books and technology that enrich, educate and empower every individual in our city's diverse communities.

lines.line: # Introduction and Canoga Park Branch

> The interview begins with an introduction of the participants and the setting. The speaker discusses the history of the Canoga Park branch, originally known as the Owensmouth branch, and its transition as the area changed names. The conversation covers the move to Sherman Way, the sale of the old property, and some unique architectural features of the new building. The speaker also briefly mentions their extensive experience moving libraries and the process involved in relocating the Canoga Park branch.

lines.line: # Chatsworth Branch

> Attention shifts to the Chatsworth branch, highlighting its mission-style architecture and nod to the local equestrian community, including a place to tie up horses. The speaker describes the tear-down and rebuild on the same site, the building's large windows, and the inviting courtyard. The branch's design and community features are discussed, along with some logistical aspects of the move.

lines.line: # Chinatown Branch

> The discussion moves to the Chinatown branch, which began as a partnership with the Castelar Street school, marking the city's first collaboration with a school system for library services. The branch eventually outgrew the school location, prompting the construction of a new building on Ord Street. The speaker recounts the opening day, which featured drummers whose performance caused a decorative beam to fall, and reflects on the importance of the new, larger facility for the community.

lines.line: # Moving Libraries and Moving Logistics

> The conversation delves into the logistics of moving library collections, including the use of machine carts to speed up the process. The speaker describes working with moving crews, particularly a trusted crew chief, and the evolution of moving techniques that reduced the time required for relocations from weeks to days. The flexibility of the library department in hiring movers and managing contracts is also discussed, along with anecdotes about managing moving crews and ensuring efficient, sober work.

lines.line: # Storage and Bookmobile Units

> The topic shifts to the storage of library materials during moves, with references to the Rio Vista warehouse and Anderson Street as key storage sites. The speaker explains the closure of bookmobile units due to budget cuts and the rationale behind reallocating resources to new branches in underserved areas. The conversation also touches on the logistics of moving materials across the city and the repurposing of facilities like the inner city bookmobile unit.

lines.line: # Cypress Park Branch and Richard Henry Dana

> The speaker discusses the Cypress Park branch, formerly known as the Richard Henry Dana branch, and its architectural style designed to blend into the residential neighborhood. The conversation covers the building's history, its transformation into a community center, and the move to a new Spanish-influenced building. The speaker also shares a personal connection with the artist who created the mural in the new branch and reflects on the significance of author-named branches from the 1920s.

The speaker also shares a personal connection with the artist who created the mural in the new branch and reflects on the significance of author-named branches from the 1920s.

lines.line: # Will and Ariel Durant Branch (West Hollywood)

> The discussion turns to the Will and Ariel Durant branch, originally the West Hollywood branch, and the process of renaming it after West Hollywood became its own city. The speaker recounts debates over the order of the names and the eventual decision to honor the couple as they were known in their published works. The move from the Gardner location to the current site on Sunset is described, along with logistical details about the building and its parking lot.

lines.line: # Security and Moving Experiences

> The speaker describes the security measures taken during library moves, including personally managing access to closed buildings and dealing with the public's attempts to enter or return books. Anecdotes include interactions with notable individuals, such as mistakenly turning away Gloria Kaufman, and the general experience of keeping construction sites safe. The speaker emphasizes the importance of politeness and the lack of major security incidents during these transitions.

lines.line: # Eagle Rock Branch

> The conversation moves to the Eagle Rock branch, which has remained in its 1970s building. The speaker recalls the old Carnegie building, its brief service before structural issues, and its subsequent use as a community arts center. Anecdotes include attempts to retrieve forgotten items from the old site and the building's appearances in films and community events. The architectural and historical significance of the branch is also discussed.

lines.line: # Carnegie Buildings and Branch Architecture

> The discussion broadens to Carnegie library buildings, their standardized floor plans, and regional architectural variations. The speaker notes the influence of local styles, such as mission and Tudor, and the financial requirements for matching Carnegie grants. The process of researching historical property records and grant deeds is mentioned, highlighting the importance of preserving documentation for future reference.

lines.line: # Edendale Branch and Numbering Error

lines.line: # Volunteerism and Changing Work Culture

> The discussion shifts to the culture of volunteerism among library staff during moves, with many employees from other branches willingly helping with packing and relocation. The speaker laments a generational shift away from this spirit of cooperation, attributing it to changing mindsets and a decline in volunteer motivation. The importance of public service and the joy of helping others are emphasized as core values of library work.

lines.line: # Encino-Tarzana Branch

> The conversation moves to the Encino-Tarzana branch, which underwent a tear-down and rebuild on the same site. A memorable story is shared about a rainstorm during the move, which revealed a leak in the new building and required quick adaptation to protect the collection.

lines.line: # Exposition Park Branch (Expo)

> The discussion focuses on the Exposition Park branch, formerly known as the University branch, and its various relocations. The speaker recounts issues with the parking lot being used for event parking during games at the Coliseum, including an anecdote about unauthorized parking sales. The move to the new LEED-certified building is described, along with the careful relocation of the historic Charles White mural. The branch's relationship with nearby USC and its role in the community are also mentioned.

The speaker discusses the history of the Canoga Park branch, originally known as the Owensmouth branch, and its transition as the area changed names. The conversation covers the move to Sherman Way, the sale of the old property, and some unique architectural features of the new building. The speaker also briefly mentions their extensive experience moving libraries and the process involved in relocating the Canoga Park branch.
# Chatsworth Branch

> Attention shifts to the Chatsworth branch, highlighting its mission-style architecture and nod to the local equestrian community, including a place to tie up horses. The speaker describes the tear-down and rebuild on the same site, the building's large windows, and the inviting courtyard. The branch's design and community features are discussed, along with some logistical aspects of the move.
# Chinatown Branch

> The discussion moves to the Chinatown branch, which began as a partnership with the Castelar Street school, marking the city's first collaboration with a school system for library services. The branch eventually outgrew the school location, prompting the construction of a new building on Ord Street. The speaker recounts the opening day, which featured drummers whose performance caused a decorative beam to fall, and reflects on the importance of the new, larger facility for the community.

story.title: LAPL Institutional Collection - Robyn Myers Pt 3
story.description: Robyn Myers, Management Analyst is interviewed by Jim Sherman, Librarian II in the Literature & Fiction Department. This third interview with Robyn was conducted on August 20, 2025 the Octavia Lab at Central Library.
